insights.insight: Ad tech charges $5–$150 CPA for modeled attribution. This protocol settles at roughly $0.10 per cryptographically verified receipt — a 50–200x cost difference that isn't a negotiating position, it's a structural gap in how purchase proof is produced.

Chrome's cookie deprecation completes the collapse of cross-site tracking across all major browsers. The protocol produces identity-free, cross-ecosystem purchase signals without cookies, pixels, or lookback windows — the exact primitive the industry lost and hasn't replaced.

insights.topic: Receipt as conversion event, not proxy
insights.insight: 3,703 verified receipts represent confirmed purchase events, not behavioral inferences. The receipt IS the conversion record — no modeled attribution, no probabilistic match. That's a different category of signal than anything current ad tech sells.

The dataset has reached rural and secondary markets across Maine, Maryland, New York, Pennsylvania, and Massachusetts — regions where probabilistic targeting has the least data density and the lowest accuracy. Verified receipts from these markets are proportionally scarcer and more valuable.

Stripe, Apple, and Google receipts all produce the same spend token in this protocol regardless of payment rail. That cross-ecosystem view doesn't exist anywhere else — every major platform sees only its own slice of a buyer's spend.

Five developer infrastructure vendors — Notion, Render, Vercel, DigitalOcean, Hetzner — are pending DKIM allowlist approval. SaaS subscription receipts are the most predictable and recurring purchase signal in the dataset; each approved domain opens a new verified spend category.
